data.title: 'I have no retirement savings': My wife and I are in our 40s. She has an $85K Roth IRA. Is it too late for me?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Historically, we've been keeping the contribution to her Roth at $50-$200 a month'

"Would it still make sense to start a retirement account, or should I just do the regular brokerage account, buy into the S&P 500 and keep things liquid?" (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

My wife and I are in our early 40s. She has a Roth with $85,000; I have no retirement savings. We're a few months away from paying away all high-interest debt, and when that happens, we'll be focusing on retirement savings and/or investing.

I'm unsure whether or not I should open up an IRA, just because I feel like I'm entering the game a little too late and the magic of compounding interest wouldn't happen at this point. Would it still make sense to start a retirement account, or should I just do the regular brokerage account, buy into the S&P 500 and keep things liquid?

Or should we be laser-focused on maxing my wife's Roth, and dump any extra into savings/investing? Historically, we've been keeping the contribution to her Roth at $50-$200 a month, depending on our situation at the time while paying off our debt.

What suggestions do you have?

The 40-something Husband

Related: My mother has dementia. If I force her into memory care without a conservatorship, would that be considered kidnapping?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

You will benefit from compounding whether you put money into an S&P 500 index fund or invest in a Roth IRA or 401(k).

Dear 40-something,

Today is the best day to start an IRA or 401(k) account. Why? Because the past is another country.

You will benefit from compounding whether you put money into an S&P 500 index fund or invest in a Roth IRA or 401(k). With a Roth IRA, you get significant tax advantages, including tax-free growth and qualified withdrawals, and no required minimum distributions (RMDs) if you are the original owner. An S&P 500 SPX index fund is an investment product that gives you suitably diversified exposure to the stock market, but while it may have more liquidity, you will still have to pay taxes on withdrawals.

Know the rules. If you make a Roth IRA withdrawal before the age of 591/2, you will likely have to pay a 10% early-withdrawal penalty. "Withdrawals must be taken after a five-year holding period," Charles Schwab (SCHW) says. "If you transfer your traditional or Roth IRA at any age and request that the check be made payable to you, you have up to 60 days to deposit that check into another IRA without taxes or penalties. This is known as a 'nontaxable rollover,' and you can do this once within a 12-month period."

All of those guides saying you should have [fill in the blank] saved in your 40s or 50s can be counterproductive, making people feel like they're left behind and will never catch up. But you and your wife are running your own race. Don't run anyone else's. If your wife earned a 7% annual return on her $85,000 Roth IRA, she would have $460,000 after 25 years; if she continued to contribute $200 to her Roth IRA, she'd have $612,000. If you contributed $200 a month to a Roth IRA, you would have $152,000, which would give you tax-free growth if you withdrew it in retirement.

Options like 401(k)s lie ahead

There may be other options open to you and your wife over the next two-plus decades of work. They include a work-sponsored retirement plan like a 401(k), which often comes with matching contributions from an employer (known in the business as "free money"). So much can change between now and then, and you probably won't hit your peak earning capability until your 50s. You will also have Medicare when you turn 65 and Social Security at 67. In the meantime, make sure you have at least a six-month emergency fund to prevent yourself from going into debt again.

Members of the Moneyist's Facebook (META) group weighed in on your letter, some sharing their own experiences. "I contributed to a 401(k) at an employer," one person wrote. "I went to work when I was 43. I worked for them for 12-13 years. I kept the account, but kind of ignored it since it 'wasn't much' - it was about $181,000 when I left in 2010. In 2020, when I decided to retire, it had grown to about $550,000 through the 'how did that happen!' method. (I totally ignored it.) It has since grown to over $700,000 although I was taking some distributions for a while. So, yes, you should start at any age. You are not too old."

So much can change before your retirement. You probably won't hit your peak earning capability until your 50s.

The longer you hold out for Social Security, the better, especially if you expect to live into your 80s. If you retired at 62, for example, a $1,000 retirement benefit would be reduced by 30%, according to the Social Security Administration. Every year you delay claiming past "full retirement age" (67 in your case) will add 8% to that benefit until you reach the age of 70. That may seem like a long way off now, and it is - but the older you get, the faster the years pass you by. The average retirement savings for someone age 35 to 44 is $141,500.

As Edward Jones says: "The relevant data point isn't what others your age have saved but how much money you need yourself. The answer depends almost entirely on you, your habits now and your plans for later. For example, what's your average monthly spending today and do you expect to maintain it after retirement? Do you expect to relocate? If so, will you live in a region where the cost of living is higher or lower than where you are now? How do you plan to spend your time?"

Have faith. You're far too young to give up on your retirement.

Related: I retired at 61, but went back to work. Was I right?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions for publication here, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

'I gave up a job that I loved passionately': My husband secretly set up a trust that includes our home and his investments. What should I do?

'He thinks nothing of spending $40K on a new car': My husband gives me the silent treatment. How can we agree on finances?

'I don't wish to be petty': My mom, 94, is leaving her $1 million estate to me, my brother and his daughter. Is that fair?

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

data.title: Textron Aviation Enhances Parts Shipment Visibility With Sensos Smart Labels Technology
data.description:
Textron Aviation Enhances Parts Shipment Visibility With Sensos Smart Labels Technology

Textron Aviation Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company, today announced the integration of Sensos Smart Labels into its parts distribution process, offering customers the option of enhanced visibility and real-time tracking of their parts shipments. The new capability is part of Textron Aviation’s ongoing investment in digital tools and customer support innovation. By leveraging Sensos’ advanced technology, customers may purchase access to real-time location data and shipment status updates for select parts orders, improving transparency and streamlining maintenance planning.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251010471925/en/

Textron Aviation announced the integration of Sensos Smart Labels into its parts distribution process, offering customers the option of enhanced visibility and real-time tracking of their parts shipments. (Textron Aviation)

“Our customers rely on timely and accurate parts deliveries to keep their aircraft flying,” said Brad White, senior vice president, Global Parts Distribution. “By incorporating Sensos Smart Labels into our logistics network, we are providing customers the option to have an even greater confidence and control over their parts shipments—furthering our commitment to delivering the best aviation experience.”

Sensos is a leading provider of advanced package tracking solutions. Sensos Smart Labels are lightweight, wireless trackers that provide continuous location updates throughout the shipping journey. Participating customers will receive live tracking information, enhancing proactive planning and reducing downtime. Whether a customer is shipping domestically or internationally, Sensos smart Labels offer transparency and reliability to keep customers' operations running smoothly. The Sensos labels are available for purchase for part orders through Textron Aviation Parts website.

About Textron Aviation Parts

Textron Aviation offers worldwide parts availability and service programs designed to lower predictable maintenance costs. From purchasing parts to warranty claims and managing your ProAdvantage programs, we make it easy. Textron Aviation Parts is renowned for its unrivaled global network of seven parts distribution centers, 17 stockrooms and worldwide online parts website. With over 150,000 unique part numbers and a dedicated team of more than 600 professionals, Textron Aviation Parts is well-equipped to support its customers' parts needs worldwide. Find additional information about Textron Aviation’s global parts network at https://ww2.txtav.com/Parts/.

About Sensos

Sensos is a global leader in AI-powered supply chain visibility and tracking solutions. Its smart, wireless labels and advanced analytics and execution platform deliver real-time location updates, autonomous monitoring, and predictive insights—empowering organizations to reduce delays, streamline logistics, and protect high-value shipments. Trusted by industries including aviation, pharmaceuticals, and consumer electronics, Sensos is redefining how goods move through complex global supply chains. Sensos Smart Labels are available for purchase in part orders via the Textron Aviation Parts website.

About Textron Aviation

We inspire the journey of flight. For more than 95 years, Textron Aviation Inc., a Textron Inc. company, has empowered our collective talent across the Beechcraft, Cessna and Hawker brands to design and deliver the best aviation experience for our customers. With a range that includes everything from business jets, turboprops, and high-performance pistons, to special mission, military trainer and defense products, Textron Aviation has the most versatile and comprehensive aviation product portfolio in the world and a workforce that has produced more than half of all general aviation aircraft worldwide. Customers in more than 170 countries rely on our legendary performance, reliability and versatility, along with our trusted global customer service network, for affordable and flexible flight. For more information, visit www.txtav.com | www.defense.txtav.com | www.scorpionjet.com.

About Textron Inc.

Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services. Textron is known around the world for its powerful brands such as Bell, Cessna, Beechcraft, Pipistrel, Jacobsen, Kautex, Lycoming, E-Z-GO, and Textron Systems. For more information, visit: www.textron.com.

Certain statements in this press release may project revenues or describe strategies, goals, outlook or other non-historical matters; these forward-looking statements speak only as of the date on which they are made, and we undertake no obligation to update them. These statements are subject to known and unknown risks, uncertainties, and other factors that may cause our actual results to differ materially from those expressed or implied by such forward-looking statements.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Oil Falls $2.50 on Trump's Tariff Threat on China, Gaza Deal — OPIS
data.description:
Crude oil contracts fell more than $2/bbl by midday Friday, following President Trump's latest threat to impose higher tariffs against China and the prospect of a Gaza ceasefire agreement.

At 12:25 p.m. ET, November NYMEX West Texas Intermediate was down $2.50 to about $59/bbl and December WTI was also lower by the same amount to $58.55/bbl.

London-based December ICE Brent was $2.40 lower to around $62.8/bbl and January Brent was down $2.35 to $62.4/bbl.

November NYMEX RBOB was down 5.9cts to $1.8235/gal and December RBOB was 5.8cts lower to $1.7795/gal. November ULSD was down 7.3cts to $2.2075/gal and December ULSD was 7.15cts lower to $2.19/gal.

Petroleum futures accelerated losses on demand fears, after President Trump on Friday threatened to raise tariffs and impose export controls on China and said there was "no reason" to meet with Chinese leader Xi Jinping after Beijing's new restrictions on rare-earth materials marked an escalation in tensions between the countries.

The oil market finished lower on Thursday on lower geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. On Friday, the Israeli military said a ceasefire in Gaza went into effect at noon local time, setting the stage for the release of the remaining hostages and an influx of humanitarian aid into the territory. 




  This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 





  --Reporting by Frank Tang, ftang@opisnet.com; Editing by Cory Wilchek, cwilchek@opisnet.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 12:52 ET (16:52 GMT)

CRMT Investor News: Rosen Law Firm Encourages America's Car-Mart, Inc. Investors to Inquire About Securities Class Action Investigation - CRMT

PR Newswire

NEW YORK, Oct. 10, 2025

NEW YORK, Oct. 10, 2025 /PRNewswire/ --

Why: Rosen Law Firm, a global investor rights law firm, announces an investigation of potential securities claims on behalf of shareholders of America's Car-Mart, Inc. (NASDAQ: CRMT) resulting from allegations that America's Car-Mart, Inc. may have issued materially misleading business information to the investing public.

So What: If you purchased America's Car-Mart, Inc. securities you may be entitled to compensation without payment of any out of pocket fees or costs through a contingency fee arrangement. The Rosen Law Firm is preparing a class action seeking recovery of investor losses.

What to do next: To join the prospective class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=46025https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=39889or call Phillip Kim, Esq. toll-free at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for information on the class action.

What is this about: On September 4, 2025, during market hours, Benzinga published an article entitled "America's Car-Mart Stock Plunges After Sales Volume Dip, Delinquency Uptick." The article stated that America's Car-Mart, Inc. stock was trading "lower after the company reported first-quarter results. The company reported a first-quarter loss of 69 cents per share, compared with a net loss of 15 cents per share in the year-ago period."

On this news, America's Car-Mart, Inc. stock fell 18.2% on September 4, 2025.

Why Rosen Law: We encourage investors to select qualified counsel with a track record of success in leadership roles. Often, firms issuing notices do not have comparable experience, resources, or any meaningful peer recognition. Many of these firms do not actually litigate securities class actions.  Be wise in selecting counsel. The Rosen Law Firm represents investors throughout the globe, concentrating its practice in securities class actions and shareholder derivative litigation. Rosen Law Firm achieved the largest ever securities class action settlement against a Chinese Company at the time. At the time Rosen Law Firm was Ranked No. 1 by ISS Securities Class Action Services for number of securities class action settlements in 2017. The firm has been ranked in the top 4 each year since 2013 and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors. In 2019 alone the firm secured over $438 million for investors. In 2020, founding partner Laurence Rosen was named by law360 as a Titan of Plaintiffs' Bar. Many of the firm's attorneys have been recognized by Lawdragon and Super Lawyers.

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/the-rosen-law-firm, on Twitter: https://twitter.com/rosen_firm or on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/rosenlawfirm/.

data.title: BLS Prepares September CPI for Release on Oct. 24
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

The Bureau of Labor Statistics will release its September consumer-inflation data later this month despite the government shutdown, the agency confirmed Friday.

The September CPI report, originally scheduled for publication next week, will be published on Friday, Oct. 24 at 8:30 a.m. ET, the BLS said. The data were originally scheduled for next week, but the federal budget impasse left the BLS without funding and sent its staff home. Workers have been recalled to facilitate the report's release about a week late, however, because the September inflation numbers are important for calculating cost-of-living adjustments that determine social-security benefits.

In a statement, the BLS said that the September pricing data will remain an exception and that it won't publish any other data until the shutdown is resolved.

The shutdown has already delayed other major economic releases, including the September jobs report which was skipped last Friday.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

October 10, 2025 12:39 ET (16:39 GMT)

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Toronto Stocks Retreat; Aritzia Shares Jump on 2Q Results
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese 




  Toronto stocks were lower in midday trading of the last day of the week, ahead of a long weekend. Most sectors were trending lower, led by commercial services, energy and health tech stocks. Of the few gainers, the materials, retail and communications sectors were the strongest. 



  Turning to the economy, unemployment in Canada held steady in September at 7.1%, as hiring rebounded strongly following two straight months of heavy job losses. About 60,400 jobs were added last month, according to Statistics Canada. 



  Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index fell 1% to 29972.38, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 declined by 1% to 1764.33. 



  Leading the retail sector, Aritzia's stock rose 10% to 87.59 Canadian dollars ($62.47) after reporting late Thursday that revenue rose in the second quarter by nearly a third, thanks to the Canadian women's clothing retailer's rising sales in the U.S. and Canada. 





  Other market movers: 



  MTY Food Group shares fell by 7.7% to C$34.86 after reporting lower third-quarter profit as sales softened, particularly in the U.S. market, which weighed on the restaurant franchiser's results. 



  Bitfarms' Toronto-listed stock rose 10% to C$6.45 a share after the energy and digital infrastructure company said it has converted a private debt facility for up to $300 million project-specific financing and has plans to draw down $50 million to develop a data center campus in Pennsylvania. 





  Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 12:23 ET (16:23 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The Week in Oil: Gaza Deal, U.S. Government Shutdown in Focus
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni 




  Here is a look at what happened in oil markets in the week of Oct. 6-10 and what the focus will be in the days to come. 





  OVERVIEW: Oil prices are on track for a weekly loss of more than 2% after Israel and Hamas agreed to a U.S.-brokered plan for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. Brent crude trades at $63 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate is under $59 a barrel. Both benchmarks are also pressured by prospects of a looming supply surplus in global markets. 





  MACRO: The U.S. federal government shutdown entered its 10th day on Friday, with no immediate resolution in sight. The stalemate added a fresh layer of uncertainty to markets, delaying the release of key labor and inflation data. Still, markets continue to price in two more interest-rate cuts before the end of the year. 



  Meanwhile, the latest U.S. data revealed consumer sentiment held broadly stable in October, with high prices and weakening job prospects as top concerns. 





  GEOPOLITICAL RISKS: The geopolitical risk premium reduced significantly after the Israeli government approved a deal to establish a cease-fire in Gaza and release hostages held by Hamas, pushing oil prices more than 2.5% lower on Friday. 



  "These are serious sticking points, and only by rigorously following through with the U.S. proposal can the current, welcome breakthrough evolve into a permanent peace," analysts at PVM said. "Until then, oil will continue to react sensitively to headlines." 



  At the same time, the U.S. imposed fresh sanctions on Iran, targeting more than 50 individuals, entities and vessels facilitating Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments. The move is the U.S.' latest effort to ratchet up pressure on Tehran to give up its uranium-enrichment program, though the country continues to export large volumes of oil. 



  Traders are also keeping a close eye on trade, with Trump saying he sees "no reason" to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping and threatening a "massive increase" of tariffs on Chinese goods over a dispute on rare-earth minerals. 





  SUPPLY AND DEMAND: Concerns over an impending supply glut continue to weigh on sentiment, with market watchers expecting prices to fall further in the coming months. Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories rose for a second straight week, sending bearish signals about demand in the world's largest oil consumer. 



  "The fact that prices have so far only reacted with relatively moderate declines is attributable, on the one hand, to the looming risk of sanctions and, on the other hand, to high imports by China, which is likely to significantly increase its strategic oil reserves," analysts at DNB Carnegie said. 



  The Energy Information Administration estimates that crude oil inventories in China grew by roughly 900,000 barrels a day this year through August, essentially acting as a source of demand by absorbing barrels from global markets. 





  WHAT'S AHEAD: Focus remains on the U.S. government shutdown, as even more economic data could be delayed, and any signals supporting prospects of further interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. Traders will also keep an eye on inflation data in Europe, trade figures from China and the political situation in France following the surprise resignation of Prime Minister Sebastien Lecornu. 



  Next week, OPEC and the International Energy Agency will release their monthly reports with updated forecasts for supply and demand. The cartel is expected to reaffirm its optimistic forecast, with little impact on the market, according to analysts. The IEA, instead, already projects a significant oversupply. 





  Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 10, 2025 12:13 ET (16:13 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Bond market flashes signs of worry over shutdown-induced economic damage
data.description:
By Vivien Lou Chen

The 10-year Treasury yield fell below its recent trading range during Friday's session

A view of the U.S. Capitol in Washington is shown as the U.S. government's partial shutdown stretched into its 10th day on Friday.

More than a week after the U.S. government's partial shutdown began on Oct. 1, bond-market traders were starting to express a greater degree of concern about the economic fallout.

Roughly a quarter of a million federal workers missed paychecks this week and another 2 million are expected to miss them next week if the shutdown continues, said Chris Low, chief economist at FHN Financial in New York, citing a report from Bloomberg. The impact on these workers and a continued void in U.S. economic data were among the factors being mentioned by various market participants during Friday's trading session. In addition, a report from the University of Michigan showed Americans have soured on the prospects of finding new work and are frustrated by inflation.The 10-year yield fell below its recent trading range of between 4.07% to 4.17%, and was, at one point, down by as much as 9.1 basis points to a session low of just below 4.06%. Before the yield touched Friday's intraday low, Gennadiy Goldberg, head of U.S. rates strategy at TD Securities, said: "We are trending toward the bottom of that range on lots of uncertainty, given the lack of economic data. It's hard for investors to have strong views" given a current lack of conviction.

Friday's rally in U.S. government debt was strongest in intermediate- and long-dated maturities. The 30-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD30Y fell as much as 9.2 basis points to as low as 4.64%. Meanwhile, all three major U.S. stock indexes DJIA SPX COMP turned lower in New York trading, as President Donald Trump threatened to impose "a massive increase" of tariffs on imports from China. The government shutdown stretched into its 10th day on Friday, with Congress deadlocked on a funding plan, and is expected to continue next week. Some furloughed workers have been called back to work to produce the September consumer-price-index report, which should be released later this month.

-Vivien Lou Chen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires
